metadata.title: Carbohydrate Calculator - Daily Carb Intake | FitMetrics
metadata.description: Calculate your daily carbohydrate intake target using WHO-aligned carbohydrate energy ranges, your activity level, and your weight goal.

title: Carbohydrate Calculator
tagline: How many carbs should you eat per day?
shortDesc: Get a daily carbohydrate target in grams based on calorie needs, activity, and WHO-aligned ranges.
importance: Carbohydrates are the body and brain's primary fuel source for most daily activity and training. Getting carb intake right improves workout performance, energy stability, and long-term adherence to nutrition plans.
description: This calculator estimates your daily carbohydrate target from your calorie need. First, it calculates BMR using Mifflin-St Jeor, then estimates TDEE from activity level, adjusts calories for your goal, and converts carbohydrates into grams using 4 kcal per gram. It provides a practical single estimate at 55% carbohydrate and also shows the WHO-compatible range in grams.
formula:
Step 1 - BMR (Mifflin-St Jeor):
  Male:   10xW + 6.25xH - 5xA + 5
  Female: 10xW + 6.25xH - 5xA - 161

Step 2 - TDEE: BMR x Activity Factor

Step 3 - Goal calories: TDEE + Goal Delta

Step 4 - Carbohydrate grams:
  Carbs (g/day) = [Calories x Carb %] / 4
note: Public-health guidance also emphasizes carbohydrate quality, not only quantity: prioritize whole grains, legumes, vegetables, and fruit. WHO recommends keeping free sugars below 10% of total energy intake and, for additional benefit, below 5%.
source: WHO Healthy Diet Fact Sheet (2024) · WHO guideline on carbohydrate intake for adults and children (2023) · Mifflin-St Jeor (Am J Clin Nutr, 1990)

healthRisks.title: Why Your Carbohydrate Target Matters
healthRisks.intro: Both too little and too much carbohydrate can affect metabolic health, performance, and appetite control. Targeting an appropriate range supports consistency and outcomes.

overweightItems.desc: Very low carbohydrate intake can reduce training quality and increase fatigue in active individuals, making plans harder to sustain over time.
High intake of sugar-rich and ultra-processed carbohydrates can increase total energy intake and worsen glycemic control when quality is poor.
Low-fiber carbohydrate choices are linked to poorer satiety and higher cardiometabolic risk. WHO recommends at least 25 g/day of naturally occurring dietary fiber for adults.

healthRisks.underweightNote: Carbohydrate planning should always be interpreted with total calories, protein, fats, micronutrients, and individual clinical needs.

limitations.title: How to Use This Calculator Most Effectively
limitations.intro: Treat this result as a practical starting point and refine it using real-world progress and health markers:

items.title: Use the midpoint as a start, then personalise
items.desc: This calculator provides one practical estimate at 55% carbohydrate and also shows the WHO-compatible gram range. If needed, adjust within the range based on your activity and response.

items.title: Prioritize carbohydrate quality
items.desc: Whole grains, pulses, fruit, and vegetables provide fiber and micronutrients that improve satiety and metabolic outcomes compared with refined sugars.

items.title: Adjust every 2-4 weeks
items.desc: As body weight and activity change, calorie needs shift. Recalculate regularly and adjust by 25-50 g/day if progress, recovery, or hunger are not aligned with your goal.

faq.q: How many carbs should I eat daily?
faq.a: A useful public-health range is about 45-75% of daily energy intake, then converted to grams using 4 kcal per gram of carbohydrate. Your exact target depends on total calorie needs and activity.

faq.q: Why does this calculator ask for age, height, weight, and activity level?
faq.a: These inputs are used to estimate calorie needs (BMR and TDEE). Carbohydrate grams are then calculated as a percentage of that calorie target, so better energy estimation gives better carb targets.

faq.q: Are low-carb diets always better for fat loss?
faq.a: Not always. Fat loss is primarily driven by sustained energy deficit and adherence. Different carb levels can work if calories, protein adequacy, and food quality are well managed.
Usually yes. As training intensity and duration increase, carbohydrate demand rises to support performance, glycogen restoration, and recovery.

faq.q: Should I count only net carbs?
faq.a: For general health planning, total carbohydrates with attention to fiber quality is usually more practical. If you follow a specific medical protocol, follow your clinician's advice.

faq.q: How much fiber should I aim for?
faq.a: WHO guidance supports at least 25 g/day of naturally occurring dietary fiber in adults, ideally from whole-food carbohydrate sources.

faq.q: Can this replace medical nutrition advice?
faq.a: No. This is an educational estimate. For diabetes, pregnancy, kidney disease, gastrointestinal disorders, or medication-dependent conditions, use clinician-guided nutrition planning.
